justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Fulfillment, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly analyzed in escalations (multi-driver zone partitioning, cancellation as cross-context trigger) and in the bounded_context narrative. Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7). 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated as three events (BR-3, BR-4, BR-5) with cancellation flagged as a missing fourth.

observations.text: Four well-defined bounded contexts with clear ownership descriptions and explicit note that contexts must communicate via integration events, not direct coupling

Excellent invariant classification with thorough justifications — BR-1 (vehicle class) correctly true, BR-7 (stop duration) correctly false, and route recalculation triggers (BR-3/4/5) reasonably classified as false with sound reasoning
9 acceptance criteria covering shift start, all three recalculation triggers, vehicle class filtering, notification window, operations override, and the recalculation-notification interaction
10 edge cases identified including notification oscillation, concurrent triggers, mid-delivery recalculation, and vehicle swap — demonstrates deep domain thinking
5 escalations with concrete options and recommendations, surfacing critical ambiguities (optimization objective, deviation detection, cancellation, multi-driver partitioning, vehicle class taxonomy)

Cross-context dependencies could be more formally structured — the narrative mentions contexts must communicate via events but doesn't produce an explicit integration-point map (e.g., 'Delivery Fulfillment emits DeliveryFailed, consumed by Route Planning')

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3/4/5) marked invariant:false is debatable — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's reasoning (optimization policy, not data integrity) is defensible but diverges from the expected answer
Rich ubiquitous language glossary with 12 precisely defined terms grounding the domain model

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification); cross-context dependencies explicitly analyzed in escalations and edge cases (e.g., route recalculation triggered by delivery lifecycle events, vehicle class as cross-context constraint); correct invariant classification for vehicle class (BR-1: true) and stop time (BR-8: false); 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria well above the 4 minimum; route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery, failure, deviation) in BR-4/5/6.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough bounded context identification with 4 distinct contexts named and their intersections described
9 high-quality acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases, and the notification deduplication concern
Invariant classifications are all correct — vehicle class and zone scoping as true invariants, recalculation triggers/stop time/notification window as false
Cross-context dependencies surfaced explicitly in escalations (multi-driver partitioning, deviation definition) with concrete options and recommendations for MJ
10 edge cases identified including concurrency, zone boundary changes, and shift-end behavior — goes well beyond minimum requirements
Rich ubiquitous language glossary with 12 well-defined domain terms

Cross-context data contracts not stated as explicitly as the rubric envisions — the dependency between Route Planning and Delivery Lifecycle is implied through event names but not formalized as 'these contexts must share a data contract'
Confidence level appropriately calibrated at 52 with clear reasoning about spec gaps, distinguishing high vs low confidence areas

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — e.g., vehicle class constraint spans Fleet and Delivery contexts, recalculation triggers span Route Planning and Delivery Management, notifications depend on route state changes. Invariant classification correct: vehicle class (BR-1) is true, stop duration (BR-8) is false, notification window (BR-7) is false. 8 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). All three recalculation triggers (new delivery, failed delivery, route deviation) correctly enumerated in BR-4/5/6.

observations.text: Excellent bounded context decomposition with 4 clearly named and scoped contexts, each with defined ownership boundaries

Cross-context dependencies are well-analyzed — vehicle class enforcement spanning Fleet and Delivery contexts is explicitly called out, and recalculation as an event-driven cross-context interaction is clear
9 business rules identified with thorough invariant justifications — goes well beyond the minimum requirements
8 acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases (AC-6 vehicle class mismatch during recalc), and idempotency (AC-5 no duplicate notifications)
10 edge cases identified including recalculation storms, notification idempotency, and shift-end behavior — demonstrates deep domain thinking
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the multi-driver zone ambiguity and deviation definition

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) marked as invariant: false, but the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (policy could change to batch updates) is reasonable but diverges from rubric expectation
Rich ubiquitous language section with 12 precisely defined terms — strong domain modeling foundation
Confidence level appropriately conservative at 52 with well-articulated assumptions and low-confidence areas
